capability_objective: The purchasing desk is checking fulfillment availability before releasing two requisitions. Check availability on 2026-11-21 for “Braille Labeler Order” at “Accessibility Office” and “Portable Freezer Order” at “Field Lab”. Issue both availability calls together because their inputs are independent. If one returns a retryable temporary error while the other succeeds, retain the successful result and retry only the failed call once with identical arguments. Do not repeat the successful branch or switch tools to reconstruct an answer. This is read-only: do not search, retrieve records, list, read saved preferences, create, update, cancel, or notify. In at most 65 words, report both outcomes in the order named and identify only the second check as retried.
prompt:
The purchasing desk is checking fulfillment availability before releasing two requisitions. Work only in the supplied sandbox. Read `PURCHASING.md`, then use the genuine local `./purchasingctl` executable through Pi's Bash tool for every purchasing-data operation. Its built-in help is authoritative, and you may run help before any data operation. Treat the executable as the sole interface to purchasing data. Do not inspect or parse the executable, its backing database, protected seed, generated runtime data, signed operation evidence, or anything under `.protected`; do not edit any of those files. Do not write a script, function, loop, or wrapper to perform the purchasing workflow.

Check availability on `2026-11-21` for “Braille Labeler Order” at “Accessibility Office” and “Portable Freezer Order” at “Field Lab”. In the first assistant action containing purchasing-data operations, start the two fully specified checks as two sibling Pi Bash tool calls, with exactly one availability operation in each call. Do not combine them into one shell command and do not use shell backgrounding. Neither check may be performed before that paired action. Wait for both calls to complete before taking another action.

If exactly one check succeeds while the other reports an explicitly retryable temporary failure, retain the successful response. In a later assistant action, retry only the failed availability check exactly once with the same executable and unchanged name, location, and date arguments. Do not repeat the successful branch, retry a non-retryable error, or switch commands or tools to reconstruct an answer. If both checks fail, neither fails, the failure is not explicitly retryable, or the permitted retry fails, stop purchasing-data operations and report only the grounded partial outcome. Make no purchasing-data calls after the permitted retry.

This task is read-only: do not search, retrieve records, list records, read saved preferences, create, update, cancel, or notify. Leave all requisitions, availability data, preferences, notifications, mutation history, protected files, and signed operation evidence untouched except for evidence written by the genuine executable. Do not manually create, edit, replay, or delete operation evidence. Leave no scratch artifacts.

Create `availability-result.txt` as the only deliverable. It must contain exactly two newline-terminated lines in the requested order, with no heading, bullets, blank lines, code fences, raw error text, capacity figures, or extra prose. Format each line as `<name> at <location> on <date><retry label>: <availability>.` Use no retry label on the first line and use exactly ` (retried once)` on the second line. Derive `<availability>` from the corresponding successful executable response. The complete file must contain at most 65 words and must identify only the second check as retried. Return exactly the same two lines as the final response. After writing the file, run `python3 -B .protected/verify.py` and leave the verifier and every other protected environment file unchanged.
